runs.response:
Snipara's tech stack (from the provided docs):

- Frontend: Next.js 14, Tailwind, DaisyUI, Tremor (Tech Stack)  
- Backend: Next.js API Routes, tRPC (Tech Stack)  
- MCP server: Python FastAPI (Tech Stack)  
- Database: PostgreSQL + Prisma (Tech Stack)  
- Auth: NextAuth.js (Tech Stack)  
- Billing: Stripe (Tech Stack)  
- Cache / rate limiting: Redis (Tech Stack)  
- Deployment: Vercel (web) + Railway (MCP) (Tech Stack)

Additional integration details also listed: pgvector for vector search and Resend or SMTP for email (External Dependencies & Integration Points).

Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service — i.e., dramatically shrinking and prioritizing client documentation so clients can get useful LLM answers without sending huge context. Specifically:

- “90% context reduction — From 500K tokens to ~5K tokens of highly relevant content.” (Project Overview — Core Value)  
- Clients “use their own LLM — No vendor lock-in, no LLM costs for us.” (Project Overview — Core Value)  
- High-margin, compute-only service (“High margins — Pure compute, no LLM costs passed through”). (Project Overview — Core Value)

Key insight: “The real value of RLM is not the LLM response—it's the context optimization that makes the LLM response possible within token limits.” (Product Philosophy — Key Insight)

- Total: "39 MCP Tools" — broken down as "25 Context tools + 14 Agent tools." (MCP Tools Reference: "Total: 39 MCP Tools (25 Context tools + 14 Agent tools)")

- Primary/context entrypoint: rlm_context_query — "Purpose: Return optimized context for a query within token budget." (Primary Tool: `rlm_context_query`)

- Memory tools: rlm_remember, rlm_recall, rlm_memories, rlm_forget. (MCP Tools for Memory — table and example usage)

- Shared-context tools (examples listed): rlm_shared_context, rlm_list_templates, rlm_get_template, rlm_list_collections, rlm_upload_shared_document. (MCP Tools for Shared Context — table)

- MCP server tools (when using as MCP server): execute_python, get_repl_context, set_repl_context, clear_repl_context. (MCP Tools (when using as MCP server) — table)

- Coverage status: "Summary: 33 covered, 5 partial (client method but no command), 1 missing (no client method)." Partial tools named: rlm_stats, rlm_read, rlm_list_collections, rlm_upload_shared_document, rlm_settings. Missing tool: rlm_request_access. (Snipara MCP Tools (39 total) — Summary)

If you want a full detailed list of all 39 tool names, Not found in provided context (the context does not enumerate all 39 tool names).

Short answer — there are separate pricing tiers for the Context product and for Agents. Exact values are given in multiple sections of the provided context:

- Context Plans (Context Optimization): FREE ($0), PRO ($19/mo), TEAM ($49/mo), ENTERPRISE ($499/mo) (see "Context Plans (Context Optimization)" table).  
- Products & Prices — Context Subscription: PRO ($29/mo, product id `prod_context_pro`), TEAM ($79/mo, product id `prod_context_team`), ENTERPRISE (Custom, product id `prod_context_enterprise`) (see "Products and Prices" → Context Subscription).  
- Agents Subscription: STARTER ($9/mo, `prod_agents_starter`), PRO ($29/mo, `prod_agents_pro`), TEAM ($79/mo, `prod_agents_team`) (see "Products and Prices" → Agents Subscription).  
- Note: the "1. Create Products & Prices" section also instructs creating Pro $19/mo and Team $49/mo products in Stripe (see "1. Create Products & Prices").

If you want, I can list the query/month limits or product IDs referenced in each section.

Summary: Snipara enforces a token budget by counting tokens with tiktoken, ranking candidate sections by relevance, and greedily selecting whole sections until the budget would be exceeded — truncating the last section at a sentence boundary if needed. (See "4.1 Token Budgeting & Metadata Response" and "Context Query Implementation".)

Key points:

- Token counting and encoder
  - Uses tiktoken with the cl100k_base encoding for accurate token counts. (See "4.1 Token Budgeting & Metadata Response" — Implementation Notes; and "Context Query Implementation" code: import tiktoken / get_encoding("cl100k_base").)
  - tiktoken is added to the server pyproject with tiktoken>=0.5.0. (See the Prompt in "4.1 Token Budgeting & Metadata Response".)

- API / parameters
  - The rlm_context_query tool accepts a max_tokens parameter (default 4000), plus query, search_mode (keyword|semantic|hybrid), include_metadata, and optional session_id. (See "4.1 Token Budgeting & Metadata Response" Tasks and "Primary Tool: `rlm_context_query`" — Request example.)

- Selection algorithm
  - Candidate sections are ranked by relevance, then selected greedily (highest relevance first) while summing token counts and stopping when the budget would be exceeded. (See "4.1 Token Budgeting & Metadata Response" Tasks #3 and the "Context Query Implementation" code.)
  - Token counts for each section are computed by encoding the section content with the cl100k_base encoder before selection. (See "Context Query Implementation" code: section_tokens = len(encoder.encode(section.content)).)

- Partial sections and smart truncation
  - If only part of a section fits, the engine uses smart_truncate to cut to the remaining token budget, truncating at a sentence boundary (split on . ! ?) and appending "..." and marking the section metadata truncated: true. (See "4.1 Token Budgeting & Metadata Response" Tasks #4 and the "Context Query Implementation" code and Implementation Notes.)

- Returned metadata / response shape
  - The response includes per-section metadata: title, content, file path, line range, relevance_score, token_count, and truncated flag when applicable. It also returns total_tokens, truncated (overall), session_context (if any), and suggestions. (See "4.1 Token Budgeting & Metadata Response" Implementation Notes and the "Primary Tool: `rlm_context_query`" — Response example and the "Context Query Implementation" response construction.)

- Session context and system injection
  - Session context can be included in the response (and implementation notes state session context inclusion up to 20% of the budget); system instructions may be auto-injected. (See "4.1 Token Budgeting & Metadata Response" — Implementation Notes.)

- Higher-level budget/allocation patterns
  - There are category-based allocation defaults and priority rules (e.g., MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE with percentage allocations), and a separate budget-allocation implementation that allocates budgets per category and within categories greedily by relevance. (See "Token Budget Algorithm" and "5.1 Budget Allocation Logic".)
  - Hard limits and collection-level enforcement (e.g., MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION) are also enforced in the allocation logic. (See "5.1 Budget Allocation Logic" — LIMITS and enforceCollectionLimits.)

If you want, I can show the exact request/response example from the docs or walk through how a specific query would be budgeted step‑by‑step using the greedy selection and smart truncation rules. (See "Primary Tool: `rlm_context_query`" and "Context Query Implementation".)

- What they are:
  - "Reusable collections of coding standards, best practices, and guidelines shared across projects or teams." (SharedContextCollection; "Team Best Practices with Shared Context Collections")

- Purpose / benefits:
  - Make team coding standards automatically available to all linked projects so the AI and developers follow the same rules. ("Shared Context Collections allow teams to create reusable coding standards, best practices, and guidelines that are automatically available to all team projects." — Team Best Practices with Shared Context Collections)
  - Enable enforcing non‑negotiable rules (security/architecture) and surfacing recommended patterns (code style, testing) to every query. (Document Categories table — Team Best Practices with Shared Context Collections)

- How they’re organized:
  - Documents are categorized by priority: MANDATORY (highest), BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE; each category has an associated token budget (40% / 30% / 20% / 10%). (Document Categories table — Team Best Practices with Shared Context Collections)

- Creation & linking workflow (summary):
  1. Team admin creates a Shared Context Collection. (TEAM SHARED CONTEXT WORKFLOW — Team Best Practices with Shared Context Collections)
  2. Upload documents (example: rlm_upload_shared_document used to upload standards). (Creating Shared Context Collections)
  3. Link the collection to projects in the dashboard; linked projects automatically inherit the collection documents. (Creating Shared Context Collections; Share Context Across Projects)

- Runtime behavior / APIs:
  - rlm_shared_context returns merged shared context from linked collections (you can filter categories and set max_tokens) and the system distributes tokens across categories. (Shared Context Tools; Using Shared Context in Development)
  - When you run project queries (rlm_context_query), shared context is automatically included and token budget is split (example: 60% project docs + 40% shared context). (Using Shared Context in Development)
  - You can load shared context at session start and inject it into session context so subsequent queries MUST follow the standards. (Workflow Example: Team-Aligned Feature Implementation)
  - Additional tools: rlm_list_templates and rlm_get_template expose prompt templates stored in collections. (Shared Context Tools; Prompt Templates)

- Team features:
  - Multi-project search across all team projects is supported via rlm_multi_project_query. (Multi-Project Search — Team Best Practices with Shared Context Collections)
  - UI supports linking, reordering (priority), and conflict handling in the Project Shared Context Manager. (Project Shared Context Manager)

- Plan / access:
  - Team-wide shared collections and auto-injection require the TEAM plan (or ENTERPRISE); not available on Free/Pro. (👥 Team Standards (Auto-Enforce Rules); Access Levels table)

If you want, I can point to the exact code examples or API parameter snippets shown in the context (e.g., rlm_upload_shared_document, rlm_shared_context parameters, or the ProjectContextManager component).

Short summary
- Snipara implements the standard OAuth 2.0 Device Flow (RFC 8628) so users can authenticate without copying API keys: "OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628) enables secure MCP authentication without copying API keys." (OAuth Device Flow (MCP Authentication))

How it works (step‑by‑step, with server behavior)
1. The user starts authentication from the CLI, then "visit[s] a URL, enter[s] a code, and authenticate[s] via browser." (OAuth Device Flow (MCP Authentication))
2. The MCP client polls the token endpoint to obtain the access token: POST /api/oauth/device/token with a body like:
   {
     "grant_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
     "device_code": "abc123...",
     "client_id": "snipara_cli"
   }
   (POST /api/oauth/device/token)
3. While the user hasn’t completed authorization the endpoint returns a pending response:
   { "error": "authorization_pending" }
   (POST /api/oauth/device/token)
4. When authorization succeeds the endpoint returns an OAuth access token (example):
   {
     "access_token": "snipara_at_...",
     "token_type": "Bearer",
     "expires_in": 2592000,
     "refresh_token": "snipara_rt_..."
   }
   (POST /api/oauth/device/token)
5. Token handling and validation:
   - Token format uses the "snipara_at_" prefix and validation is done by DB lookup + expiry check; scopes are enforced. (1.2 OAuth Token Authentication)
   - The MCP server checks for a stored OAuth token in ~/.snipara/tokens.json first, and falls back to an API key from environment variables if none is found. (MCP Server Authentication)
6. If an OAuth token is invalid or expired, the server replies with an HTTP 401 message instructing re‑authentication: "Invalid or expired OAuth token. Re-authenticate at https://snipara.com/dashboard or run /snipara:quickstart". (Invalid OAuth Token (HTTP 401))

If you need the exact request/response examples or the token‑format/validation lines, see the POST /api/oauth/device/token and 1.2 OAuth Token Authentication sections cited above.

Snipara uses a layered architecture with a strict import hierarchy. From the "TypeScript Architecture Layers" section:

"Import hierarchy (lower layers cannot import from higher):

```
Routes (app/api/)
  ↓ imports
Services (lib/services/) — External API adapters
  ↓ imports
Repositories (lib/db/queries/) — Centralized CRUD only
  ↓ imports
Utils & Types — No dependencies
```"

Route handlers are thin and delegate to services/queries, and all DB access goes through the repository layer (lib/db/queries/) per the "Route Handler Pattern" and "Repository Pattern" sections.

- Use rlm_context_query when you have a single, direct documentation/code question and you want an optimized, semantic context return (it "return[s] optimized context for a query within token budget") — see "Primary Tool: `rlm_context_query`".

- Use rlm_decompose when the question is complex and should be split into parts — it is for "Break[ing] complex questions into sub-queries" and when you "want to inspect/modify sub-queries before running" (see "Multi-Query for Complex Tasks" and "POWER USER TOOLS" → `rlm_decompose`).

- Common workflow (from the docs): run rlm_decompose to produce sub-queries, then run the sub-queries in parallel with rlm_multi_query (see "Multi-Query for Complex Tasks").

According to the provided context:

Definitions (from "Search Modes"):
- "keyword" — "Basic term matching" (Search Modes).
- "semantic" — "Embedding similarity" (Search Modes).
- "hybrid" — "Combined (best results)" (Search Modes).

Recommended use by scenario (from "When to Use Each Mode" table):
- Use keyword for:
  - "Looking for specific function" (When to Use Each Mode).
  - "Error code lookup" (When to Use Each Mode).
  - "API endpoint discovery" (When to Use Each Mode).
- Use semantic for:
  - "'How does X work?' questions" (When to Use Each Mode).
  - "Architecture understanding" (When to Use Each Mode).
- Use hybrid for:
  - "General documentation search" (When to Use Each Mode).
  - "Best practices search" (When to Use Each Mode).

Short answer
- Shared context tokens are split by category (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE) and by project vs shared docs; the system uses explicit percentage budgets to decide which documents to include. (See "Document Categories" table; "Shared Context Tools"; "MCP Query Order (Shared Context First)".)

How it is applied (details from the provided docs/code)
- Category percentages within shared context:
  - MANDATORY: 40%
  - BEST_PRACTICES: 30%
  - GUIDELINES: 20%
  - REFERENCE: 10%
  (See "Document Categories" table and the shared-context examples that show the same 40/30/20/10 split.)

- Project vs shared allocation (implementation-level default):
  - The token-budget implementation defines a DEFAULT_ALLOCATION where:
    - project = 0.40 (40% to project docs)
    - MANDATORY = 0.25
    - BEST_PRACTICES = 0.20
    - GUIDELINES = 0.10
    - REFERENCE = 0.05
  - Allocation code groups docs (project vs shared categories), allocates the project budget first (floor(totalBudget * allocation.project), then allocates each shared category using floor(totalBudget * allocation[category]) and fills within each category by relevance and tokenCount. (See "5.1 Budget Allocation Logic" / apps/web/lib/services/token-budget.ts.)

- Query-level behavior and parameters:
  - When using rlm_context_query, shared context is loaded first and the example shows a default shared_context_budget_percent of 30 (i.e., shared context gets 30% and project gets the remaining 70% unless overridden). The rlm_context_query parameters include "shared_context_budget_percent". (See "MCP Query Order (Shared Context First)" and the rlm_context_query parameters snippet.)

- Allocation algorithm specifics:
  - allocateWithinBudget sorts docs by relevanceScore (if available) then allocates documents until the per-category/per-project budget is exhausted; docs beyond the budget are marked allocated: false with reason "exceeded budget". (See allocateWithinBudget in "5.1 Budget Allocation Logic".)

- Hard limits and collection truncation:
  - There are hard limits such as MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION = 100_000, MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION = 50, MAX_COLLECTIONS_PER_PROJECT = 10; enforceCollectionLimits will truncate a collection’s docs to fit MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION. (See LIMITS and enforceCollectionLimits in "5.1 Budget Allocation Logic".)

Notes / apparent inconsistencies in the docs
- The docs show multiple related defaults:
  - Category-level shared-context split: 40/30/20/10 (Document Categories / Shared Context examples).
  - Implementation DEFAULT_ALLOCATION that includes a 40% project share and smaller per-category shares (0.25, 0.20, 0.10, 0.05) in the token-budget.ts code.
  - MCP Query Order example that uses shared_context_budget_percent: 30 (shared gets 30%).
- All of the above are present in the provided context; the exact percentages used at runtime depend on which parameter or code path is in effect (e.g., rlm_context_query's shared_context_budget_percent, or the DEFAULT_ALLOCATION in token-budget.ts). (See "Document Categories", "5.1 Budget Allocation Logic", and "MCP Query Order (Shared Context First)".)

Not found in provided context

The provided context describes the architecture (two codebases and shared DB), product lines (Context Plans and Agents Plans), RLM runtime features, and that Snipara Agents is a separate product section in the dashboard, but it does not include any information about real-time collaborative editing or how to configure it. (See: "Snipara consists of two separate codebases..." and "Snipara has two product lines..." and "Snipara Agents is integrated as a separate product section within the dashboard..." in the provided context.)
"RLM" stands for "Recursive Language Models." (See "Naming: What 'RLM' Stands For" section.)

The MCP server requires Python 3.10 or newer — see the "Snipara MCP Server" section which shows a "Python 3.10+" badge. The "3.1 MCP Server Setup" notes the Dockerfile uses a multi-stage Python 3.12 build.

- Per the "What Happens When Limits Are Exceeded" section: when a user exceeds their monthly query limit the API returns a 429 Too Many Requests response with a body like:
  {
    "success": false,
    "error": "Monthly query limit exceeded",
    "usage": {
      "current": 101,
      "max": 100,
      "exceeded": true,
      "reset_at": "2026-02-01T00:00:00Z"
    }
  }
  (source: "What Happens When Limits Are Exceeded")

- The same section documents the `usage` fields:
  - `current` (int): current usage this billing period  
  - `max` (int): maximum allowed queries (-1 for unlimited)  
  - `exceeded` (boolean): whether the limit has been reached  
  - `reset_at` (string): ISO 8601 timestamp when the limit resets (monthly)  
  (source: "What Happens When Limits Are Exceeded" — field table)

- Related examples in the context:
  - A per-minute rate-limit response example uses an error with code "RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED" and message "Rate limit exceeded. Try again in 60 seconds" (source: "429 Rate Limited").
  - The "Query Limits" section shows an alternate error example when credits are insufficient:
    {
      "error": {
        "code": "INSUFFICIENT_CREDITS",
        "message": "Need 5000 credits, have 5000"
      }
    }
  (sources: "429 Rate Limited" and "Query Limits")

- The handler initializes selected_sections = [] and total_tokens = 0, so if no candidates are chosen the response will contain no sections and zero tokens used (see "Context Query Implementation" code where selected_sections and total_tokens are initialized).  
- The final response uses those values directly: "sections": selected_sections, "total_tokens": total_tokens, and "truncated": total_tokens >= max_tokens (so truncated will be false when total_tokens is 0) (see the response build in "Context Query Implementation").  
- The response will still include session context if present ("session_context": self.session_context if self.session_context else None) (see "Context Query Implementation").  
- The handler calls self._generate_suggestions(query, selected_sections), so the response may include generated suggestions even when no sections are returned (see the response build in "Context Query Implementation").  
- If this is the first rlm_context_query in the session, first-query tool tips may also be included (response field first_query_tips_included) (see "First-Query Tool Tips").
